title: Linear Functions
blurb: Slope and rate of change, y = mx + b, and reading a line four ways: table, graph, equation, and words.

lessons.title: What Is a Function?
lessons.concreteComparison: A vending machine: press B4, get the same granola bar every time. Press it tomorrow — same bar. A machine that sometimes dropped chips and sometimes soda for the SAME button would be broken. A **function** is exactly that promise in math: each input gets **one** guaranteed output.
lessons.objective: Recognize a function as a rule assigning exactly one output to each input, read function tables, and spot non-functions.
concept: A **function** is a rule that takes an **input** and produces exactly **one output**. Input 3 into the rule “double it, add 1” and you always get $7$ — never sometimes $7$, sometimes $9$.
Functions often appear as **tables** of input–output pairs. The rule $y=2x+1$ makes the table $(1,3), (2,5), (3,7), (4,9)$.

**Not** every table is a function: if the SAME input appears with two DIFFERENT outputs — like $(2,5)$ and $(2,8)$ — the rule is broken. (Repeated outputs are fine; repeated inputs with different outputs are not.)
The set of inputs is what you feed the rule; each input owns exactly one output — that's the whole definition.
lessons.keyIdea: One input → exactly one output. Same input can never give two different outputs.

facts.title: The promise
facts.explanation: That's what makes a rule a function.

workedExamples.statement: The rule is $y=2x+1$. Find the output for input $x=3$.
The rule is $y=5x-2$. Complete the pair $(4, \;?)$.
A table holds $(1,4), (2,7), (3,10)$. Verify the rule $y=3x+1$ fits every row.

commonMistakes.text: Rejecting a function because two inputs share an output.
commonMistakes.correction: $(1,5)$ and $(2,5)$ is fine — different inputs may agree. Only a repeated INPUT with different outputs breaks a function.

commonMistakes.text: Treating a function as one fixed number.
commonMistakes.correction: A function is the whole RULE — the machine, not one snack. $y=2x+1$ describes every pair at once.

tryIt.statement: Rule: $y=4x-3$. What is the output for $x=5$?

steps.title: Input → rule → output
beats: Feed it an input, it returns ONE output.

steps.title: Pairs the rule produces
beats: Each row: input on the left, its one output on the right.

steps.title: When it's NOT a function
beats: Input $2$ has TWO outputs — broken machine.
different inputs may share an output.

steps.title: Check the rule
steps.intro: Substitute each input; expect its one output.

examples.prompt: Rule $y=5x-2$: find the outputs for $x=1$ and $x=4$.

problems.prompt: Rule $y=2x+5$: the output for input $6$ is…
Which table is NOT a function?
problems.explanation: Input $3$ appears with two different outputs — broken.

problems.prompt: Rule $y=x^2$: the output for input $5$ is…
problems.explanation: $5^2=25$ — still one output per input, so still a function.

steps.title: Functions run your life
steps.body: Your phone bill is a function of data used. Cooking time is a function of a turkey's weight. A song's pitch is a function of time. The idea of “one input, one guaranteed output” is so useful that all of physics is written in functions.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A function assigns each input exactly **one** output.
Tables show a function as input–output pairs.
Repeated input with different outputs = not a function.

lessons.title: Slope & Rate of Change
lessons.concreteComparison: Two ramps into a building: one rises $2$ ft over a $10$ ft run, the other rises $2$ ft over just $4$ ft. The second is much steeper — you feel it in your legs. **Slope** puts a number on steepness: rise divided by run. The wheelchair-ramp rule in many building codes is a slope of at most $\frac{1}{12}$ — one foot up for every twelve along.
lessons.objective: Compute slope as rise over run between two points, interpret it as a rate of change, and read positive, negative, zero slopes from a graph.
concept: **Slope** measures steepness: $m=\dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}=\dfrac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}$ between any two points on a line.
Slope is a **rate of change**: dollars per GB, km per hour, candies per bag. “$y$ changes by $m$ each time $x$ grows by 1.”
Signs tell the story: **positive** slope climbs left-to-right, **negative** falls, **zero** is flat (a horizontal line). On a LINE, any two points give the same slope — that's what makes it a line.
lessons.keyIdea: $m=\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}$ — how much $y$ changes per 1 step of $x$.

facts.title: Read the sign
facts.latex: up ↗ positive · down ↘ negative · flat → zero

workedExamples.statement: A ramp rises $2$ ft over a $24$ ft run. Its slope?

commonMistakes.correction: $\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}$ or $\frac{y_1-y_2}{x_1-x_2}$ both work, but mixing them ($\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_1-x_2}$) flips the sign.

steps.title: Rise over run, live
steps.teach: Drag either point. The triangle shows the **rise** and the **run**; the slope is their ratio — and it stays the same anywhere on the line.

steps.title: Two points → one number
beats: Every 3 steps right, the line climbs 2.

steps.title: Slope with units
beats: A plan costs \$3 per GB: slope $3$ dollars/GB.
A tank drains 2 L per minute: slope $-2$ L/min.
“Per” is the giveaway — slope IS the per.
Positive grows, negative shrinks, zero stays flat.

steps.title: Find the slope
steps.intro: Rise on top, run on the bottom.

problems.prompt: A climb of 3 m over a run of 12 m has slope…

steps.title: The steepest street on Earth
steps.body: Baldwin Street in New Zealand climbs about $1$ m for every $2.86$ m along — a slope of $0.35$, or $35\%$. Cars park sideways, and every year runners race straight up it. For comparison, highway builders try to keep slopes under $6\%$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Slope is a rate: the change in $y$ per 1 of $x$.
Positive climbs, negative falls, zero is flat.

lessons.title: Slope-Intercept Form
lessons.concreteComparison: A gym: $\$20$ to join, then $\$15$ a month. Total cost after $x$ months: $y=15x+20$. Two numbers tell the whole story — $15$ (the monthly rate: the **slope**) and $20$ (the starting cost: the **y-intercept**). Every line works the same way: $y=mx+b$, a rate plus a starting point.
lessons.objective: Read the slope $m$ and y-intercept $b$ from $y=mx+b$, evaluate points on the line, and match equations to their meaning.
concept: **Slope-intercept form**: $y=mx+b$. The coefficient $m$ is the **slope**; the constant $b$ is the **y-intercept** — where the line crosses the $y$-axis (at $x=0$: $y=m\cdot0+b=b$).
In $y=2x+1$: slope $2$, intercept $1$. The line starts at $(0,1)$ and climbs $2$ for every $1$ right.
In stories, $b$ is the **starting amount** and $m$ the **rate**: $y=15x+20$ is “\$20 up front, \$15 per month.”
Watch signs and missing parts: $y=x-4$ has $m=1$, $b=-4$; $y=3x$ has $b=0$; $y=5$ has $m=0$ (flat line).
lessons.keyIdea: $y=mx+b$: $m$ = rate/steepness, $b$ = where the line starts on the y-axis.

facts.title: Read it
facts.explanation: Coefficient is slope; constant is intercept.

facts.title: At x = 0
facts.explanation: The intercept is the starting value.

workedExamples.statement: For $y=2x+1$: give the slope, the intercept, and the point at $x=3$.
A phone plan: $y=10x+30$. What do the numbers mean, and what's the cost at $x=6$ months?
Is the point $(4,5)$ on the line $y=2x-3$?

commonMistakes.correction: In $y=3x+7$ the slope is the number ON $x$ ($3$); the lone $7$ is the intercept.
$y=2x-5$ has $b=-5$, not $5$ — the sign belongs to it.

tryIt.statement: In $y=4x-2$, what are $m$ and $b$? What is $y$ at $x=2$?

steps.title: m and b, live
steps.teach: This is $y=2x+1$. Adjust the slope and intercept: $b$ slides the line up and down; $m$ tilts it. Two dials control every line.

steps.title: What m and b do
beats: $m$: how fast it climbs — rise per 1 run.

steps.title: Read the equation
steps.prompt: In $y=5x-3$, the slope and intercept are…
steps.explanation: The coefficient of $x$ is the slope; the constant (with its sign!) is the intercept.

steps.title: Start plus rate
beats: $b=20$: the cost before any months pass.

steps.title: Points from equations
steps.intro: Substitute x; read m and b by position.

steps: Yes — the point satisfies the equation.

steps.title: Read and use y = mx + b

problems.prompt: A scooter rental: $\$5$ unlock + $\$2$ per km. Which equation?
problems.explanation: Rate \$2 rides on $x$; the \$5 unlock is the intercept.

steps.title: Why the letter m?
steps.body: Nobody knows for sure why slope is called $m$ — the most popular theory points to the French verb **monter**… except mathematicians can't find a French text that actually used it. It remains one of math's small unsolved mysteries. The $b$? Probably just “the next letter after a” in $ax+b$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: $b$ is the value at $x=0$ — the start.
In stories: $b$ = one-time amount, $m$ = the rate.

lessons.title: Graphing Lines
lessons.concreteComparison: GPS doesn't store a million dots for a straight road — it stores a starting point and a direction. Lines work the same: to draw $y=\frac{1}{2}x+1$, plant a dot at the intercept $(0,1)$, then walk the slope — up $1$, right $2$ — and connect. Start plus direction draws the whole line.
lessons.objective: Graph a line from slope-intercept form (start at b, walk the slope), and recognize horizontal lines and negative slopes on the grid.
concept: **To graph $y=mx+b$:** ① plant the intercept $(0,b)$; ② from there use the slope as directions — $m=\frac{1}{2}$ means up $1$, right $2$; ③ connect the dots with a straight line.
**Negative slope** walks down: $m=-2$ means down $2$, right $1$. $y=-2x+3$ starts at $(0,3)$ and falls.
**Horizontal lines**: $y=5$ has $m=0$ — no climb at all, a flat line through $(0,5)$.
Check any graph by testing a point: on $y=\frac12 x+1$, at $x=4$: $y=3$. If $(4,3)$ is on your drawn line, it's right.
lessons.keyIdea: Plant the intercept, walk the slope, connect — and verify with one substituted point.

workedExamples.statement: Graph $y=\frac{1}{2}x+1$: give two points to plot.
workedExamples.solution: Intercept $(0,1)$; slope up 1 right 2 gives $(2,2)$. Check $x=4$: $y=3$.

workedExamples.statement: Which points lie on the horizontal line $y=4$?

commonMistakes.text: Walking the slope from the origin instead of the intercept.
commonMistakes.correction: The walk starts at $(0,b)$ — for $y=2x+3$ that's $(0,3)$, not $(0,0)$.
Rise is on top: $\frac12$ = up 1, right 2. (Up 2 right 1 is slope 2.)

steps.title: Start at b, walk the slope
steps.teach: This line is $y=\frac{1}{2}x+1$: it crosses the $y$-axis at $1$ and climbs 1 for every 2 right. Change $m$ and $b$ and watch the drawing recipe play out.

steps.title: Three moves
beats: ② Walk the slope: rise, then run.
③ Connect with a straight edge.

steps.title: Second point
steps.prompt: Graphing $y=3x+2$: after the dot at $(0,2)$, the slope walk lands on…

steps.title: One equation, infinite dots
steps.body: A line's equation is a compression trick: instead of listing infinitely many points, $y=2x+1$ stores them ALL in five symbols. Computer graphics does exactly this — your screen never stores a line's points; it stores the equation and computes pixels on demand.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Dot at $(0,b)$, walk $m$ as rise/run, connect.
Negative slope walks down; $m=0$ is horizontal.

lessons.title: Proportional or Not?
lessons.concreteComparison: Apples cost $\$2$ per pound: zero pounds, zero dollars — the graph starts at the origin. A taxi costs $\$2$ per mile **plus a $\$5$ flag fee**: zero miles already costs $\$5$. Both are lines with slope $2$, but only the apples are **proportional** — doubling the pounds doubles the price. The taxi's flat fee breaks that doubling.
lessons.objective: Distinguish proportional relationships ($y=mx$, through the origin) from other linear ones ($y=mx+b$, $b\neq0$), and use the constant of proportionality.
concept: A **proportional** relationship is $y=mx$ — a line through the **origin** $(0,0)$. The slope $m$ is also called the **constant of proportionality** (unit rate).
Test a table: if every $\frac{y}{x}$ is the same number, it's proportional. $(2,4), (3,6), (5,10)$: ratios all $2$ ✓.
$y=mx+b$ with $b\neq0$ is linear but NOT proportional: $(0,0)$ isn't on it, and doubling $x$ doesn't double $y$.
Shortcut: proportional ⟺ the graph is a line through the origin ⟺ $y\div x$ is constant.
lessons.keyIdea: Proportional = $y=mx$ = a line through the origin = constant $\frac{y}{x}$.

workedExamples.statement: Is the table $(2,4), (3,6), (5,10)$ proportional? Find the constant.
Apples: $y=2x$. Cost of $7$ pounds — and is it double the cost of $3.5$… of $14$ pounds vs $7$?
workedExamples.solution: $y=2(7)=14$; doubling to 14 lb gives $28$ — exactly double. Proportional.
At $x=0$, $y=5\neq0$ — not through the origin, so no. (And $\frac{y}{x}$: $\frac{7}{1}\neq\frac{9}{2}$.)

commonMistakes.correction: Linear just means straight. Proportional additionally requires passing through $(0,0)$ — no flat fee, no head start.

commonMistakes.text: Testing with differences instead of ratios.
commonMistakes.correction: Equal JUMPS make it linear; equal RATIOS $\frac{y}{x}$ make it proportional.

steps.title: Through the origin
steps.teach: This is $y=2x$ — proportional, so the line passes exactly through $(0,0)$. Give it an intercept and the proportionality breaks.

steps.title: y = mx
beats: At $x=0$: $y=0$. The line owns the origin.
Double the input → double the output.

steps.title: Judge each one
steps.intro: Look for the origin and constant ratios.

problems.explanation: At 0 minutes you already owe \$1.50 — not through the origin.

steps.title: The unit-rate superpower
steps.body: Proportional relationships are why unit prices work: if 5 lb costs \$10, then 1 lb costs \$2, and ANY amount is just $2x$. Stores must post unit prices by law in many countries precisely so shoppers can compare proportional relationships at a glance.

lessons.title: Four Views of a Line
lessons.concreteComparison: The same phone plan can be described four ways: a sentence (“\$30 up front plus \$10 a month”), a table of months and costs, a graph climbing across the page, or the equation $y=10x+30$. Same plan, four costumes. The skill isn't picking a favorite — it's **translating** between them, because every test question hands you one view and asks about another.
lessons.objective: Translate a linear relationship between words, table, graph, and equation — and compare two functions given in different views.
concept: **Words → equation**: find the rate ($m$) and the starting amount ($b$). “\$30 plus \$10 per month” → $y=10x+30$.
**Table → equation**: the jump in $y$ per 1 step of $x$ is $m$; the value at $x=0$ (or walked back to it) is $b$. Rows $(0,30),(1,40),(2,50)$ → $y=10x+30$.
**Graph → equation**: read $b$ where the line crosses the $y$-axis; count rise/run for $m$.
**Comparing across views**: to decide which of two plans grows faster, extract each slope — whatever the costume. A table jumping $12$ per month beats a graph climbing $10$ per month.
lessons.keyIdea: Every view hides the same two numbers, m and b — learn to extract them from any costume.

facts.title: Compare by m
facts.explanation: Extract m from each view first.

workedExamples.statement: “A candle is 20 cm tall and burns 2 cm per hour.” Equation and height after 6 h?
Plan A: $y=12x$. Plan B's table jumps $(1,15),(2,25),(3,35)$. Which grows faster per month?
workedExamples.solution: B's jump is $10$ per month; A's slope is $12$. A grows faster.

commonMistakes.text: Reading a table's first y-value as b when x doesn't start at 0.
Comparing functions by a single point instead of slope.
commonMistakes.correction: The plan that's cheaper in month 1 may grow faster and overtake — compare $m$ for growth, points for a specific moment.

tryIt.statement: “A tank holds 50 L and drains 5 L per minute.” Empty when?

steps.title: The graph view
steps.teach: A plan with a $\$3$ start fee and $\$2$ per month, as a graph: it crosses the $y$-axis at $3$ and climbs $2$ per step. Reading $m$ and $b$ off a graph is just this picture in reverse.

steps.title: Table to equation
steps.prompt: A table reads $(0,7), (1,9), (2,11)$. The equation is…
steps.explanation: Jump of 2 per step ($m=2$); value 7 at $x=0$ ($b=7$).

steps.title: Translate and compare
steps.intro: Extract m and b from each costume.

examples.prompt: “A plant is 4 cm tall and grows 3 cm per week.” Equation + height at 5 weeks?
Which grows faster: $y=8x+1$, or the table $(1,10),(2,16),(3,22)$?

steps.title: Switch costumes
steps.intro: Find m and b first, always.

problems.prompt: “Bike rental: \$6 plus \$4 per hour” as an equation:
problems.explanation: Rate 4 on $x$, one-time 6 as the intercept.

problems.prompt: Line P has slope 5; line Q's table jumps 7 per step. Which climbs faster?

steps.title: You can read every costume
steps.body: Words, tables, graphs, equations — you can pull $m$ and $b$ out of any of them and translate freely.

steps.title: You've finished “Linear Functions”
steps.body: Functions, slope, $y=mx+b$, graphing, proportionality, and all four views of a line — the core language of algebra is yours. Next: **Systems of Linear Equations**, where TWO lines share one plane and their crossing point answers real questions.

practice.statement: “\$25 sign-up plus \$8 per month” as an equation; cost at 4 months?
Which grows faster: $y=6x+2$ or a table jumping 9 per step?

testYourself.statement: “A 30 cm candle burns 2 cm/h.” When is it 10 cm tall?
